summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE - EXPERT EVIDENCE – Where the applicant seeks orders that the single expert real estate valuer chosen by agreement between the parties be discharged and that another expert be appointed as the single expert – Where the respondent opposed the application – Discussion of the purpose of the Rules such that a party should only obtain expert evidence in relation to a significant issue, restricted to that necessary to resolve or determine the proceedings, without compromising the interests of justice – Where the Court is not satisfied that there is any basis for the discharge of the existing single expert or there exists any basis for the appointment of an additional expert – Application dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

The parties are engaged in contested financial and parenting proceedings which are listed for final hearing to commence on 2 February 2026. By Application in a Proceeding filed 18 December 2025, the applicant seeks orders that the single expert real estate valuer chosen by agreement between the parties be discharged and that another expert be appointed as the single expert.
The application was opposed.
At a hearing on 22 December 2025, the Court heard submissions of each of the parties and, following the completion of those submissions, dismissed the Application in a Proceeding and indicated reasons would be delivered in due course.
These are those reasons.

BACKGROUND

The parties are the joint owners of a property in Tasmania which was valued by a single expert real estate valuer on 9 May 2024 at $575,000. The valuer was engaged by the parties jointly and the property represents a substantial part of the parties’ assets. The solicitor for the applicant submitted the pool of assets has a value of approximately $900,000 and includes the parties’ home valued at $575,000 by the jointly agreed single expert, the applicant’s superannuation at approximately $120,000 and savings held by the respondent totalling approximately $129,000.
The applicant believes the home is worth more than its current value and seeks to engage a different valuer because the cost of undertaking an update is more expensive than engaging a new valuer and that according to her chosen valuer there are problems with the existing valuation. In that respect, Mr Hanlon (her chosen valuer) in an email dated 8 October 2025 identifies the following problems with the existing valuation:
The report does not comply with the Federal Circuit Family Court rules, Australian Property Institute guidelines, or relevant training.
The valuer, based in Victoria, may lack sufficient experience in the Tasmanian residential market.
Although the report states a title search was not conducted as instructed, I do not see this specified in the formal instructions from [B Lawyers] attached to the report.
Section 1.3 discusses Valuation Uncertainty, a clause initially required by professional indemnity insurers and the Australian Property Institute during the COVID-19 pandemic, which was deemed unnecessary some years ago.
In Section 3.4, the report claims the property uses a septic system, yet government UST mapping indicates it is connected to a sewer gravity reticulation main on the eastern boundary.
Sections 4.1 and 4.2 incorrectly refer to the [C City Council] as a “Shire”, a mainland term; it should be referred to as [C City Council]. The correct designation for the planning scheme is the Tasmanian Planning
Scheme – [Suburb D] Local Provisions Schedule, not the [Suburb D] Planning Scheme, highlighting the valuer's lack of local knowledge.
The valuer does not address the above-average land size or any potential development opportunities associated with it, or lack thereof, due to development restraints.
Market commentary lacks relevance to the report’s purpose and the specific property, particularly in terms of rental information.
In Section 9.1 (Direct Comparison Approach), the valuer applies a mainland methodology analysing sales on a rate per square meter of land area. At the same time, Tasmanian practices typically utilise the rate per square meter of living area approach, supplemented by a summation check.
The SWOT Analysis in Section 10 is usually required for mortgage valuations, but not for non-mortgage valuations.
Mr Hanlon also stated:
Given the time elapsed since this valuation, the local market has evolved, interest rates have been lowered, and the government has implemented a new first home buyers guarantee, contributing positively to market dynamics. I recommend reviewing any valuation older than three months.
Somewhat inconsistently with the case advanced by the applicant, she also contends that the respondent will make it difficult for any valuer to access the property and may sabotage in some way that process as well as expressing some concern for the safety of the valuer.

DISPOSITION

Rule 7.02 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) embodies the purpose of the Rules that deals with experts, namely, that a party should only obtain expert evidence in relation to a significant issue, that it should be restricted to that necessary to resolve or determine the proceedings, and that without compromising the interests of justice, expert evidence is to be given by a single expert.
Rule 7.08 imposes a restriction on a party seeking to tender a report or adduce evidence from another expert subject to the Court being satisfied pursuant to r 7.08(2) that:
(a) there is a substantial body of opinion contrary to any opinion given by the single expert witness and the contrary opinion is or may be necessary for determining the issue; or

(b) another expert witness knows of matters, not known to the single expert witness, that may be necessary for determining the issue; or

(c) there is another special reason for adducing evidence from another expert witness.

I am not satisfied that there is any basis for the discharge of the existing single expert. Nor am I satisfied that there exists any basis for the appointment of an additional expert.
There is no adequate explanation as to why, aware of the issues raised in the email of 8 October 2025, the applicant has waited over two months to bring this application and where it is brought about six weeks before the final hearing is to commence. The solicitor for the applicant conceded that no questions have been directed to the single expert to address the asserted concerns raised by Mr Hanlon in his email. No aspect of the respondent’s submissions identified a substantial body of opinion contrary to the opinion expressed by the single expert or that the proposed expert knows of matters not known to the single expert that cannot otherwise be put to the existing single expert through cross-examination.
Mr Hanlon has not expressed any opinion as to value nor even ventured an estimate. There is no evidence that Mr Hanlon would come to a different opinion than that of the existing single expert or why the respondent should have to join in the costs. If the existing single expert were discharged as proposed by the applicant and there are the difficulties foreshadowed by the applicant, it could mean there is no expert evidence at the hearing.
Enquiries made with the respondent indicated that he does not oppose the existing single expert giving evidence electronically. No submission was advanced on behalf of the applicant to suggest that any of the matters that were raised as concerns by Mr Hanlon could not be put in cross-examination of the single expert.
These are the reasons for dismissal of the applicant’s application.
